attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a painless facial lesion that has been slowly enlarging over the past 10 years. He denies any itching, bleeding or ulceration of the lesion. He works as a farmer and has significant sun exposure. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a large, irregular, pigmented macule on his face. Dermoscopy reveals an annular-granular pattern, asymmetric pigmented follicular openings and red rhomboidal structures.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical and dermoscopic features highly suspicious for lentigo maligna or lentigo maligna melanoma. The history of slow growth over years on sun-exposed skin in an older adult is typical. The dermoscopic features described are characteristic. Typically for cases suspicious of melanoma or lentigo maligna, a complete excisional biopsy would be recommended however given this is a large lesion on the face with likely significant cosmetic outcomes of such a procedure it would be worth while confirming the diagnosis via partial biopsy before proceeding with further management such as wide local excision.

Therefore partial biopsy is the most appropriate next step given the facial location and likely large size of the lesion. This can be performed via punch, incisional, or shave technique, targeting the most suspicious area. Multiple mapped biopsies may help define the extent of the lesion. This approach allows histological diagnosis while avoiding potentially unnecessary extensive facial surgery. Of the available options some of the pros and cons of each partial biopsy technique are:

  • A punch biopsy provides dermis and may provide subcutaneous tissue for assessment of tumour thickness, but there is a limited width of the sample, so this method is prone to diagnostic error. Multiple punch biopsies or mapped out punch biopsies may reduce this error, and can help define the extent of the lesion, which may be appropriate in this case.

  • An incisional biopsy removes as much of the lesion as deemed feasible by the clinician or the most invasive or suspicious part, which may be appropriate in this case.

  • A broad superficial shave biopsy provides a larger area for histopathology and is often useful in larger superficial lesions, but it fails to include dermis for the assessment of deeper part of the lesion. It would not be appropriate in this case where lentigo maligna melanoma cannot be ruled out (invasion into the dermis).

  • Frozen section and cytological analysis are inappropriate for suspicious pigmented lesions, therefore it is not an appropriate method of partial biopsy in this case.

Complete excisional biopsy would be ideal for smaller lesions suspicious of melanoma, but would likely result in significant facial deformity in this case given the size and location as discussed above. If melanoma is confirmed on partial biopsy, definitive wide local excision can then be planned appropriately.

Observation only is inappropriate for a suspicious pigmented lesion that requires histological diagnosis to exclude melanoma.

Imiquimod cream is only recommended when both surgery and radiotherapy are not possible or are refused by the patient and after confirmation of diagnosis. It should not be used without first obtaining a histological diagnosis.

Radiotherapy may be considered as definitive treatment for confirmed lentigo maligna (not melanoma) when surgery is not possible, but histological diagnosis must be established first.
learningPoints: Lentigo maligna melanoma typically presents as a slowly enlarging pigmented lesion on sun-exposed areas in older adults.
Dermoscopic features of lentigo maligna include asymmetric follicular openings, annular-granular pattern, and rhomboidal structures.
Partial biopsy is appropriate for large lesions suspicious of melanoma or lentigo melanoma where complete excision would cause significant cosmetic deformity.
references: Oakley A. Lentigo maligna and lentigo maligna melanoma. DermNet. Updated August 10, 2022. Accessed September 22, 2022. https://dermnetnz.org/topics/lentigo-maligna-and-lentigo-maligna-melanoma#:~:text=Lentigo%20maligna%20is%20an%20early,particularly%20the%20nose%20and%20cheek.

Cancer Council. Clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis and management of melanoma. Cancer Council. Updated May 24, 2018. Accessed September 22, 2022.  https://wiki.cancer.org.au/australia/Clinical_question:Effective_interventions_to_improve_outcomes_in_lentigo_maligna%3F

Therapeutic Guidelines. Melanoma. Therapeutic Guidelines. August 2022. Accessed September 22, 2022. https://tgldcdp-tg-org-au.eu1.proxy.openathens.net/viewTopic?etgAccess=true&guidelinePage=Dermatology&topicfile=c_DMG_Considerations-in-the-use-of-topical-corticosteroids_topic_1&guidelinename=Dermatology&sectionId=c_DMG_Melanoma_topic_2#c_DMG_Melanoma_topic_2
Ungureanu L, Vasilovici AF, Trufin II, et al. Lentigo Maligna Treatment—An Update. J. Clin. Med. 2024, 13, 2527
